According to data from the FORRT Library of Reproduction and Replication Attempts (FLoRA), there are 2 replication attempts of a finding from this study. Future research may learn from replications and build on them. Manual inspection of these articles may help to assess what led to the described outcome.

Replication article reference: Chen, W., Dong, B., Hsieh, C., Lee, M. J., Liu, N., Walker, R. M., Wang, Y., Wen, B., Wen, W., Wu, P., Wu, X., & Zhang, J. (2022). A replication of ---an experimental test of the <scp>expectancy---disconfirmation</scp> theory of citizen satisfaction.--- Public Administration, 100(3), 778---791. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/padm.12860
Outcome: "successful"
Outcome quote: We found support for the findings reported in Van Ryzin (2013) and concluded that the expectancy-disconfirmation model holds promise in a variety of settings as a framework for measuring citizen satisfaction with public services.
Quote source: "abstract"

Replication article reference: Grimmelikhuijsen, S., & Porumbescu, G. A. (2017). Reconsidering the expectancy disconfirmation model. Three experimental replications. Public Management Review, 19(9), 1272---1292. https://doi.org/10.1080/14719037.2017.1282000
Outcome: "mixed"
Outcome quote: Study 1 and 2 reproduced original findings. In contrast, study 3 indicates that expectation cues are retrospectively used to anchor prior experiences of performance.
